Were You Asked This?
Design Uber

Design a ride-sharing service like Uber that allows users to request rides, matches them with nearby drivers, handles real-time tracking, and processes payments.

Asked at:

Google

Salesforce

Visa

Oracle

+4 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Uber is a ride-sharing marketplace that connects riders who need transportation with nearby drivers using their personal vehicles. Users request a ride, get matched to a driver in real time, watch the car approach on a map, take the trip, and pay seamlessly in-app.

Interviewers ask this question to test your ability to design a low-latency, geo-distributed, event-driven system that balances real-time data (driver locations), matching logic under contention, and a multi-step payment flow. Strong answers show you can decompose the product into services, choose the right data models and indexes for location queries, handle unreliable mobile networks, and reason about correctness with eventual consistency and idempotency.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown

System design answer key for designing a ride-sharing service like Uber, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to request a ride, receive an upfront price/ETA, and get matched with a nearby driver.

Users should be able to track the driver’s approach and the trip in real time, and communicate with the driver.

Drivers should be able to go online/offline, share live location, accept/decline requests, and navigate to pickup/drop-off.

Users should be able to pay in-app (including tips) and see trip history and receipts; both parties should be able to rate the trip.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you handle frequent driver location updates and efficient proximity searches at city scale?

How do you design the driver-dispatch algorithm to avoid double-assignments and handle contention during surge?

How would you deliver real-time trip updates (positions, ETAs, status) reliably on flaky mobile networks?

How do you design the end-to-end payments flow (quote, hold, capture, tips, refunds) with consistency and idempotency?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Ride tracking, driver approach, and status changes must reach mobile clients in sub-second latency despite flaky networks. A real-time updates pattern (WebSockets/MQTT, fan-out, backpressure, fallbacks) is essential to deliver location deltas and trip events reliably.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Matching riders to a small set of nearby drivers creates hotspots and race conditions, especially during surge. You need sharding by geo-cell, short-lived reservations, and idempotent state transitions to avoid double-assignments and ensure fairness.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

The trip lifecycle and payments involve multiple external calls (quote, hold, capture, refund, payout) that can fail independently. Sagas or workflow orchestration with compensating actions keep the system consistent without relying on distributed transactions.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis provides in-memory speed, geospatial indexes (GEOADD/GEORADIUS or equivalents), and primitives like SETNX with TTL for driver reservations. It’s a natural fit for hot driver presence, proximity lookups, and short-lived locks in the dispatch path.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka decouples high-rate location ingest from consumers, supports ordering within partitions (e.g., per driver or geo-cell), and powers event-driven trip lifecycles, analytics, ETA models, and replay. It’s the backbone for reliable, scalable streaming in this domain.

Read More
DynamoDB

DynamoDB offers low-latency, horizontally scalable storage for operational entities (users, drivers, trips) with high write throughput and global tables for multi-region presence. It pairs well with Kafka and Redis to persist the system of record for trips and payments.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Local Delivery Service

Local delivery also matches supply to demand by location, tracks couriers in real time, and orchestrates multi-step flows (dispatch, pickup, drop-off, payment). The dispatching, geo-partitioning, and real-time client updates are closely related.

Read
Practice
Strava

Fitness tracking ingests frequent GPS updates from many mobile devices and renders live maps. While not a marketplace, it shares the challenges of location data ingestion, smoothing, storage, and real-time streaming to clients.

Read
Practice
Payment System

End-to-end payment processing (holds, captures, refunds, idempotency) mirrors the ride billing flow. Understanding gateways, outbox, sagas, and reconciliation directly transfers to the financial side of ride-sharing.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Using a single relational database scan for nearest-driver queries without a geo index or caching layer

This leads to high latency and poor scalability as driver counts grow. Without geo-partitioning and an in-memory spatial index, proximity lookups become the bottleneck and fail to meet real-time SLAs.

Ignoring contention and idempotency in dispatch (e.g., assigning the same driver to multiple riders)

Lack of atomic reservations and idempotent state transitions causes double-assignments, rider timeouts, and driver confusion, especially during surge. The system becomes unstable under load.

Treating payments as a single step without holds, idempotency keys, or compensations

Real gateways are flaky; without a saga and outbox, you risk double charges, orphaned holds, and mismatched payouts. This erodes user trust and creates costly reconciliation issues.
